_meta.authoringNotes: ESID 2019 diagnostic criteria for CVID (Bonilla et al. J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract 2016, PMID 26563668). Treatment recommendations from IDSA/AAAAI 2015 (Perez et al. PMID 26452416) and Mayo Clinic Proceedings 2019. Survival data from Resnick et al. Blood 2012 (PMID 22039258). USIDNET registry data. Subcutaneous Ig efficacy from Berger et al. J Clin Immunol 2008.

heroOverview: Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) is the most frequent symptomatic primary antibody deficiency, defined by low serum IgG together with low IgA or IgM, poor antibody response to vaccines, and recurrent bacterial infections starting in late childhood or adulthood. Population estimates place prevalence at roughly 1 in 25,000-50,000 in Europe and North America, with most patients diagnosed between ages 20 and 45 after a mean diagnostic delay of 4-9 years. Patients typically present with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, autoimmune cytopenias, chronic diarrhoea, granulomatous disease, or lymphoma. Untreated CVID leads to progressive lung damage, severe infections, and a 4-fold increase in lymphoma risk. Lifelong immunoglobulin replacement therapy by intravenous or subcutaneous route reduces serious bacterial infections by approximately 70-80% and extends life expectancy to near-normal in patients without major non-infectious complications.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 25,000-50,000 in Europe and North America; lower in East Asia; rising recognition with awareness
keyStats.demographics: Affects both sexes equally; can present at any age but two peaks: ages 1-5 and 20-45
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of diagnosis 25-35 years; mean diagnostic delay 4-9 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 250,000+ diagnosed cases globally; USIDNET registry tracks over 4,000 patients in the United States

definition: Common variable immunodeficiency (ICD-10: D83.0-D83.9; principal code D83.9) is a heterogeneous primary immunodeficiency characterized by impaired B-cell differentiation into antibody-producing plasma cells, resulting in low total IgG (more than 2 standard deviations below age-matched normal) combined with low IgA or low IgM, poor antibody response to T-cell-independent and -dependent vaccines (pneumococcal polysaccharide and tetanus or diphtheria toxoid), and absence of other defined causes of hypogammaglobulinaemia. The European Society for Immunodeficiencies (ESID) 2019 criteria require age over 4 years, exclusion of secondary causes (drug-induced, protein-losing states, malignancy), IgG below 2 SD with low IgA or IgM, and either poor vaccine response or low switched memory B-cells. CVID is genetically heterogeneous; monogenic causes are identified in 15-25% of cases (TACI/TNFRSF13B, ICOS, CD19, CTLA4, LRBA, NFKB1, NFKB2, PIK3CD), while most cases are polygenic and influenced by environmental factors. Beyond infections, CVID is recognized as a syndrome of immune dysregulation: 20-30% develop autoimmune disease (immune thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, autoimmune thyroid disease), 10-20% develop granulomatous and lymphocytic interstitial lung disease, 10-15% develop enteropathy with chronic diarrhoea and malabsorption, and lifetime lymphoma risk is approximately 4-fold higher than the general population. CVID is managed by allergists/immunologists with multidisciplinary input from pulmonology, gastroenterology, haematology, and infectious disease.

typesClassification.description: Approximately 50% of patients present with recurrent infections alone, predominantly sinusitis, otitis, bronchitis, and pneumonia. Best response to immunoglobulin replacement and lowest mortality.

20-25% develop immune thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, or neutropenia. May precede the infection phenotype. Often requires steroids, rituximab, or splenectomy in addition to immunoglobulin replacement.

typesClassification.type: CVID with granulomatous-lymphocytic interstitial lung disease (GLILD)
typesClassification.description: 10-20% develop granulomas in the lungs, lymph nodes, spleen, liver, and bone marrow. GLILD causes restrictive lung disease and reduces life expectancy. Treatment combines rituximab and azathioprine in addition to immunoglobulin replacement.

10-15% develop chronic diarrhoea, weight loss, and malabsorption resembling coeliac, lymphocytic colitis, or Crohn-like disease but unresponsive to gluten exclusion. Requires nutritional support and immunosuppression.

typesClassification.type: CVID with lymphoid hyperplasia and malignancy
typesClassification.description: Lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly common; lifetime lymphoma risk roughly 4× general population. Gastric cancer also elevated. Routine surveillance is recommended.
15-25% of patients have an identifiable single gene defect (e.g., NFKB1, LRBA, CTLA4, PIK3CD). Genetic diagnosis informs targeted therapy (sirolimus, abatacept, leniolisib) and family counselling.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent or chronic sinusitis, otitis media, bronchitis, and pneumonia caused by encapsulated bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae) — typical from late childhood or early adulthood.
Chronic productive cough, sputum production, and recurrent chest infections with bronchiectasis on high-resolution CT in 30-50% of long-standing cases.
Persistent or recurrent diarrhoea (more than 2 weeks per year), abdominal cramping, malabsorption, and weight loss in approximately 10-15% of patients.
Symmetrical or asymmetrical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and rarely hepatomegaly from lymphoid hyperplasia.
Easy bruising, mucosal bleeding, petechiae, or unexplained anaemia from immune thrombocytopenia or autoimmune haemolytic anaemia.
Recurrent skin or soft-tissue infections, conjunctivitis, or pyoderma in some patients.
Persistent gingivitis, dental caries, and oral candidiasis from impaired mucosal immunity.
Granulomatous skin lesions, hepatic granulomas with cholestasis, or pulmonary granulomas causing breathlessness.
Failure to thrive, growth faltering, and delayed puberty in pediatric-onset CVID.
Severe or atypical infections including Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, mycobacterial infection, and disseminated viral disease, especially in combined cellular immune defects.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections requiring more than 4 antibiotic courses per year
Two or more episodes of pneumonia in childhood or adulthood
Failure to clear a sinus or chest infection completely between flares
Family history of primary immunodeficiency or unexplained childhood death from infection
Unexplained chronic diarrhoea, weight loss, or malabsorption
Persistent low IgG, IgA, or IgM on routine bloodwork
emergencySigns: Severe sepsis with hypotension, organ dysfunction, or meningitis — immediate IV antibiotics and critical care
Acute haemolytic crisis with rapidly falling haemoglobin and jaundice
Massive haemoptysis or new-onset severe breathlessness in known CVID with bronchiectasis or GLILD
Sudden visual loss or focal neurological deficit suggesting CNS infection or lymphoma
Acute abdomen with signs of perforation in CVID with enteropathy
Anaphylaxis or severe systemic reaction during immunoglobulin infusion

causes.cause: Impaired B-cell differentiation into plasma cells
causes.description: Defective signalling and class switching prevent B cells from maturing into long-lived antibody-producing plasma cells. The molecular basis is heterogeneous and not always identifiable, but every form converges on inadequate IgG, IgA, and often IgM production.

Single-gene mutations in TACI/TNFRSF13B, ICOS, CD19, CTLA4, LRBA, NFKB1, NFKB2, and PIK3CD account for 15-25% of CVID. Each gene defect produces a recognizable phenotype with specific complications and informs targeted therapy (sirolimus for PIK3CD gain-of-function; abatacept for CTLA4 or LRBA deficiency).

Most CVID is polygenic, with multiple susceptibility loci and environmental triggers (infections, autoimmune disease) contributing. Genome-wide association studies have identified HLA and complement region variants associated with CVID.

causes.cause: Defective T-cell help and immune regulation
causes.description: T-cell abnormalities in CVID include reduced naïve CD4+ T-cells, raised CD8+ T-cells, and impaired regulatory T-cell function. These changes explain the autoimmune and granulomatous complications seen alongside hypogammaglobulinaemia.

First-degree relatives of CVID patients have approximately 8-10% prevalence of antibody deficiency or selective IgA deficiency, supporting heritable predisposition. Familial CVID is identified in 10-25% of cases.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of CVID or selective IgA deficiency
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of CVID patients have 8-10% prevalence of antibody deficiency. IgA deficiency may evolve into CVID in approximately 2-10% of patients over years.
Early-onset recurrent infections (more than 4 antibiotic courses per year) are the strongest clinical risk factor; lifetime risk of CVID is substantially elevated in such patients.
HLA-DR3 and certain HLA class III complement region variants are over-represented. Monogenic causes account for 15-25%.
Peak diagnostic age is 25-35 years, though presentation can occur at any age. Adult onset is more common than paediatric onset.
Patients presenting first with immune thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, or autoimmune thyroid disease should have immunoglobulin testing — CVID may be diagnosed years later.
Highest reported prevalence in European, North American, and Australian populations. Likely partly reflects diagnostic access rather than true biology.

affectedDemographics: European, North American, and Australian populations have the highest reported prevalence (1 in 25,000-50,000).
Prevalence is lower in East Asian, African, and South Asian populations, partly reflecting under-diagnosis.
Patients with chronic productive cough and bronchiectasis without other cause: approximately 5-10% have CVID.
Patients with immune thrombocytopenia or autoimmune haemolytic anaemia: 4-8% have CVID.
First-degree relatives of patients with CVID: 8-10% have antibody deficiency or selective IgA deficiency.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical history, immunoglobulin measurement, functional vaccine response testing, and exclusion of secondary causes. The ESID 2019 criteria require: age over 4 years; markedly low IgG (more than 2 SD below age-matched normal) together with at least one of low IgA or low IgM; absent or poor antibody response to vaccines (pneumococcal polysaccharide and tetanus or diphtheria toxoid, with conjugate vaccines as additional input); and exclusion of other causes including drug-induced hypogammaglobulinaemia (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies, antiepileptic drugs, sulfasalazine), protein-losing enteropathy or nephrotic syndrome, lymphoid malignancy, congenital agammaglobulinaemia, and HIV. Initial laboratory work-up includes full blood count with differential, serum immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE), IgG subclasses, lymphocyte subsets (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, CD56, switched memory B-cells), specific antibody responses to pneumococcal capsular polysaccharide and tetanus toxoid (pre- and 4-week post-vaccination titres), urinalysis for protein, faecal calprotectin and faecal alpha-1-antitrypsin if enteropathy is suspected, HIV serology, and serum protein electrophoresis. Imaging includes high-resolution CT of the chest at baseline and every 2-3 years to detect bronchiectasis and granulomatous-lymphocytic lung disease, and abdominal ultrasound or CT to assess splenomegaly and lymphadenopathy. Genetic testing (whole exome or targeted CVID panel) is increasingly recommended, especially in early-onset disease, syndromic features, or refractory autoimmunity. Bone marrow biopsy is reserved for unexplained cytopenia or suspected malignancy. Differential diagnosis is broad and includes X-linked agammaglobulinaemia (boys with absent B-cells), hyper-IgM syndrome, selective IgA deficiency, transient hypogammaglobulinaemia of infancy, drug-induced hypogammaglobulinaemia, multiple myeloma with hypogammaglobulinaemia, and chronic lymphocytic leukaemia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms hypogammaglobulinaemia — IgG more than 2 SD below age-matched normal with low IgA or IgM defines the laboratory hallmark
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 1-2 days. Single low IgG is non-diagnostic; the pattern of involvement and exclusion of secondary causes is essential.

diagnosticTests.test: Specific antibody response to vaccines (pneumococcal polysaccharide, tetanus)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates impaired antibody production — a core ESID criterion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Baseline blood draw, immunization with pneumococcal polysaccharide (Pneumovax 23) and tetanus toxoid, repeat blood draw at 4 weeks. Inadequate fold rise confirms deficient vaccine response.

diagnosticTests.test: Lymphocyte subset analysis by flow cytometry
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, CD56 cells, switched memory B-cells, and naïve T-cells
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialist flow cytometry on EDTA blood; results in 3-7 days. Low switched memory B-cells (CD27+IgD-IgM-) support CVID and predict complications.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bronchiectasis, granulomatous-lymphocytic interstitial lung disease, lymphadenopathy, and consolidation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minute scan with low radiation. Repeated every 2-3 years to monitor progression. Baseline CT recommended at diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic testing (whole exome sequencing or CVID gene panel)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies monogenic causes (TACI, NFKB1, NFKB2, PIK3CD, CTLA4, LRBA, ICOS, CD19, others) in 15-25%
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or saliva sample; results in 6-12 weeks. Positive findings inform targeted therapy and family counselling.

diagnosticTests.test: Faecal calprotectin and faecal alpha-1-antitrypsin (when enteropathy suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies intestinal inflammation and protein loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single stool sample. Calprotectin >250 µg/g indicates active inflammation; elevated alpha-1-antitrypsin suggests protein-losing enteropathy.

diagnosticTests.test: HIV serology and serum protein electrophoresis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes acquired hypogammaglobulinaemia from HIV and from lymphoid malignancy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws. Results in 1-3 days. Essential before diagnosing CVID.

treatmentOverview: Treatment has four pillars: immunoglobulin replacement, infection management, prevention and treatment of complications, and supportive care. Lifelong immunoglobulin replacement therapy is the cornerstone. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is given at 400-600 mg/kg every 3-4 weeks targeting a trough serum IgG above 7-8 g/L (700-800 mg/dL), individualized to the patient and the prevention of breakthrough infections. Subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) is given weekly or every 2-4 weeks at equivalent total monthly dose, often using facilitated SCIG (with recombinant hyaluronidase) for larger volumes per site. SCIG offers steadier serum levels, fewer systemic adverse reactions, and home self-administration after training. AAAAI 2015 guidelines recommend immunoglobulin replacement for all patients with classical CVID and recurrent infections. Targeted antibiotic therapy is given for breakthrough infections (amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline for sinopulmonary infections; ciprofloxacin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for resistant organisms). Long-term prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin three times weekly or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) are considered in patients with bronchiectasis and frequent exacerbations. Autoimmune cytopenias are treated with corticosteroids, rituximab, intravenous immunoglobulin at higher dose (1-2 g/kg), and thrombopoietin receptor agonists. GLILD is treated with combined rituximab and azathioprine or mycophenolate after multidisciplinary discussion. Enteropathy requires nutritional support and immunosuppression. Cancer surveillance (lymphoma, gastric cancer) is routine. Genetic counselling, family screening, and consideration of haematopoietic stem cell transplant in severe monogenic CVID complete the multidisciplinary approach. Patient education on infection prevention, vaccination policy (avoid live vaccines unless approved by immunology), travel precautions, hygiene, and access to home care services is integral.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg every 3-4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line replacement therapy; infusion takes 2-4 hours in clinic or home setting. Target trough IgG ≥ 7-8 g/L, individualized to prevent breakthrough infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces serious bacterial infections by approximately 70-80% and pneumonia rate by 60-70%; near-normal life expectancy in patients without major non-infectious complications

medicalTreatments.name: Subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) 100-200 mg/kg weekly or facilitated SCIG every 2-4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Self-administered at home after training. Steadier IgG levels, fewer systemic reactions than IVIG, greater patient autonomy. Increasingly preferred for stable patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable infection prevention to IVIG with improved quality-of-life scores and reduced systemic adverse reactions

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly or co-trimoxazole 480-960 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for patients with bronchiectasis or frequent breakthrough infections despite adequate IgG trough. Watch for QT prolongation with azithromycin and resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces exacerbation frequency by 30-50% in patients with bronchiectasis

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids and rituximab for autoimmune cytopenias
medicalTreatments.description: Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with taper, followed by rituximab 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 if relapse or steroid dependence. Thrombopoietin receptor agonists for refractory ITP.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial response in 60-80% of ITP and AIHA; sustained remission in 40-60% with rituximab

medicalTreatments.name: Combined rituximab plus azathioprine or mycophenolate for GLILD
medicalTreatments.description: Rituximab 375 mg/m² × 4 doses with maintenance azathioprine 1-2 mg/kg or mycophenolate 1-3 g/day. Treats granulomatous-lymphocytic lung disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilizes or improves lung function in 60-70% of treated patients in published case series

medicalTreatments.name: Annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal conjugate vaccine
medicalTreatments.description: Inactivated vaccines are safe and may produce some response; live vaccines (BCG, MMR, varicella, yellow fever) are generally contraindicated. Vaccinate household contacts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Some protection from immunization plus passive immunity from immunoglobulin replacement

medicalTreatments.name: Targeted therapies for monogenic CVID
medicalTreatments.description: Sirolimus or leniolisib for PIK3CD gain-of-function; abatacept for CTLA4 or LRBA deficiency; rituximab/azathioprine for NFKB1/NFKB2 disease. Stem cell transplant in selected severe cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phenotype-specific responses; leniolisib reduces lymphadenopathy and improves immune function in PIK3CD activation syndrome

surgicalOptions.name: Splenectomy for refractory immune cytopenias
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for severe steroid- and rituximab-resistant immune thrombocytopenia or autoimmune haemolytic anaemia. Vaccinate against encapsulated bacteria before surgery and continue lifelong penicillin prophylaxis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term remission in 60-70% of refractory ITP; carries infection risk requiring lifelong prophylaxis

surgicalOptions.name: Haematopoietic stem cell transplant for severe monogenic CVID
surgicalOptions.description: Considered for selected patients with severe immune dysregulation from CTLA4, LRBA, or other monogenic defects refractory to targeted therapy. Carries 10-20% transplant-related mortality.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of underlying defect in 60-80% in published series; high-risk procedure reserved for severe disease

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation for end-stage GLILD or severe bronchiectasis
surgicalOptions.description: Last-resort option for selected patients with terminal lung disease and good performance status. CVID itself is not a contraindication if well-controlled.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival 50-60% in published series; comparable to other indications

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation and airway clearance
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured exercise, breathing techniques, postural drainage, and devices (Acapella, flutter valve) for patients with bronchiectasis. Reduces exacerbations and improves exercise capacity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support and probiotics for enteropathy
alternativeTreatments.description: Dietitian-led management of malabsorption with vitamin supplementation (B12, folate, vitamin D), enteral or parenteral nutrition when needed. Probiotics may help selected patients with diarrhoea but evidence is limited.

whySeeSpecialist: An allergist/immunologist or haematologist with primary immunodeficiency expertise is essential to confirm the diagnosis, plan immunoglobulin replacement, manage complications, coordinate vaccines, and provide genetic counselling. Multidisciplinary input from pulmonology, gastroenterology, infectious disease, and rheumatology is needed for organ-specific complications.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a centre with a dedicated primary immunodeficiency clinic, in-house immunoglobulin infusion service, access to home subcutaneous immunoglobulin, genetic testing for inborn errors of immunity, and links to a national or international PID registry (USIDNET, ESID). Ask about transition pathways for paediatric to adult care.
hospitalCriteria: Immunoglobulin infusion service (intravenous and subcutaneous) with home-care option
Genetic testing facility for primary immunodeficiency panel
Multidisciplinary GLILD, autoimmune cytopenia, and enteropathy services
Stem cell transplant capability for severe monogenic forms
preventionStrategies: Early diagnosis through immunoglobulin testing in patients with recurrent infections — reduces lung damage and complications.
Family screening of first-degree relatives with serum immunoglobulin levels.
Routine vaccination of household contacts and healthcare workers (annual influenza, COVID-19, pertussis).
Hand hygiene, food safety, and avoidance of close contact with people who have respiratory infections.
Air filtration and avoidance of cigarette smoke for patients with lung disease.
Pre-travel review for vaccinations (live vaccines avoided), antibiotic prescription, and water/food safety.
Cancer surveillance protocols (annual chest CT, upper GI endoscopy for high-risk patients) to detect lymphoma and gastric cancer early.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain immunoglobulin replacement adherence with infusion calendar and reminders.
Practice rigorous hand hygiene and avoid crowded indoor settings during respiratory virus seasons.
Stop smoking and avoid second-hand smoke to protect lung function.
Perform daily airway clearance (chest physiotherapy, breathing devices) if bronchiectasis is present.
Maintain a balanced diet rich in fresh fruit, vegetables, lean protein, and whole grains.
Stay physically active with daily walking, swimming, or cycling at moderate intensity.
Avoid raw or undercooked meat, soft cheeses, and unpasteurized dairy to reduce foodborne infection risk.
Inform every clinician and surgeon of the CVID diagnosis before any procedure.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruit, vegetables, oily fish, olive oil, and whole grains
Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support immune function and recovery from infection
Vitamin D, calcium, and vitamin B12 supplementation when deficient
Probiotic foods (yoghurt, kefir) in selected patients with enteropathy under specialist advice
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat, fish, eggs, and unpasteurized dairy
Soft cheeses and pâté unless heated, due to listeriosis risk
Unwashed or unpeeled fresh produce when traveling to high-risk regions
Tap water in regions with poor sanitation

exerciseGuidelines: Encourage 150 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise weekly plus resistance training twice a week. Patients with bronchiectasis benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation and daily airway clearance. Avoid swimming in untreated freshwater because of bacterial exposure risk.
dailyManagement: Maintain immunoglobulin replacement schedule and record infusion dates and any reactions.
Perform daily airway clearance for bronchiectasis (Acapella, active cycle of breathing).
Monitor temperature daily and report fevers above 38 °C to the immunology team.
Keep an updated infection diary documenting antibiotic courses, fevers, and missed work.
Take prophylactic antibiotics at the same time daily if prescribed.
Wear medical alert identification and carry an emergency information card.
Maintain dental hygiene with twice-yearly dental review.
prognosis: Outcomes have transformed since the introduction of immunoglobulin replacement. Patients with infection-only CVID have near-normal life expectancy when treatment starts before significant lung damage. Resnick et al. (Blood 2012) reported overall 35-year survival of 64% from diagnosis in adults, with non-infectious complications (GLILD, lymphoma, autoimmune cytopenia, enteropathy) being the principal cause of premature death rather than infection itself. Patients with autoimmune cytopenia, GLILD, or enteropathy have substantially higher mortality (3-11 fold). Lymphoma is approximately 4-fold more common than general population. Quality of life is affected by chronic infusions, infections, and complication management; subcutaneous immunoglobulin and home infusion programmes substantially improve patient autonomy. Earlier diagnosis (reducing the current 4-9 year delay) is the single most important determinant of better outcomes; widespread immunoglobulin testing in recurrent infections shortens the diagnostic interval.
recoveryTimeline: Infections respond to standard antibiotics; immunoglobulin replacement reduces frequency over 6-12 months. Bronchiectasis is irreversible once established but progression slows. Autoimmune cytopenias resolve over weeks to months with appropriate immunosuppression. GLILD stabilizes or improves over 6-12 months with rituximab-based therapy. Lifelong follow-up and lifelong immunoglobulin replacement remain the norm.
complications: Bronchiectasis from repeated bacterial pneumonias — diagnose early with HRCT; prevent with adequate immunoglobulin trough levels and airway clearance.
Granulomatous-lymphocytic interstitial lung disease (GLILD) — restrictive lung disease causing breathlessness; treated with rituximab plus azathioprine.
Autoimmune cytopenias (immune thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, neutropenia) — treated with corticosteroids, rituximab, IVIG, and TPO agonists.
Lymphoma (predominantly non-Hodgkin B-cell) with roughly 4× background risk — surveillance with annual examination, CT, and biopsy of new lymphadenopathy.
Gastric cancer with 10-50× background risk — consider upper GI endoscopy surveillance in high-risk patients.
Enteropathy with chronic diarrhoea, malabsorption, and weight loss — managed by gastroenterology with immunosuppression and nutritional support.
Anaphylactic or systemic reactions to immunoglobulin infusion — switch product, slow rate, or change route (IV to subcutaneous).

supportResources.name: Immune Deficiency Foundation (IDF)
supportResources.description: Leading US patient charity providing education, advocacy, peer support, and research funding for primary immunodeficiencies including CVID.

supportResources.name: European Society for Immunodeficiencies (ESID) — Patient Information
supportResources.description: European professional society with patient resources and registry information.

supportResources.name: Primary Immunodeficiency UK (PID UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient support charity with helpline and information for adults and children with primary immunodeficiency.

supportResources.name: International Patient Organisation for Primary Immunodeficiencies (IPOPI)
supportResources.description: Global federation of patient organisations advocating for CVID and other PID care worldwide.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HIV causes acquired immunodeficiency through CD4 T-cell depletion and is diagnosed by positive HIV antibody/antigen test and viral load. CVID is a primary antibody deficiency with low IgG/IgA/IgM and impaired vaccine response; HIV testing is mandatory before diagnosing CVID.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes reversible airway obstruction with wheeze, cough, and atopic features; spirometry shows bronchodilator response. CVID causes recurrent bacterial pneumonia, sinusitis, and bronchiectasis from antibody deficiency; the two can coexist but are distinct.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic sinusitis as a standalone condition involves mucosal inflammation and impaired drainage; CVID-associated sinusitis is recurrent and severe with documented immunoglobulin deficiency. All adults with chronic sinusitis should have immunoglobulin levels checked.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus is a multi-system autoimmune disease with positive ANA and anti-dsDNA. CVID can cause autoimmune cytopenias but features low immunoglobulins and recurrent infection rather than the multi-system inflammation of lupus. Some patients have features of both (granulomatous-lymphocytic interstitial lung disease overlaps).

relatedConditions.name: Bronchiectasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Major pulmonary complication of CVID from repeated bacterial pneumonia; early immunoglobulin replacement may slow progression.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Recurrent sinusitis is one of the most common presenting features of CVID; persistent disease should prompt immunoglobulin testing.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Most important differential diagnosis in adult-onset hypogammaglobulinaemia; HIV testing is mandatory before diagnosing CVID.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia is a recognized CVID complication; iron-deficiency anaemia may occur with enteropathy and malabsorption.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Some CVID patients develop overlap autoimmune features including ANA positivity; immune dysregulation links the two.

faqs.question: What is common variable immunodeficiency?
faqs.answer: Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) is the most frequent symptomatic primary antibody deficiency, defined by low serum IgG plus low IgA or IgM, poor vaccine response, and recurrent bacterial infections from late childhood or adulthood. It is treated with lifelong immunoglobulin replacement.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of CVID?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include recurrent sinusitis, otitis, bronchitis, and pneumonia, chronic diarrhoea, weight loss, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, easy bruising or anaemia from autoimmune cytopenias, and persistent fatigue. Many patients have several years of symptoms before diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is CVID diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires age over 4 years, IgG more than 2 standard deviations below normal, low IgA or IgM, poor antibody response to pneumococcal and tetanus vaccines, and exclusion of secondary causes including HIV, lymphoid malignancy, drugs, and protein-losing states. ESID 2019 criteria are the standard.

faqs.question: What causes CVID?
faqs.answer: CVID is caused by impaired B-cell maturation into antibody-producing plasma cells. About 15-25% of patients have an identifiable monogenic cause (TACI, NFKB1, NFKB2, PIK3CD, CTLA4, LRBA, ICOS, CD19). Most cases are polygenic with both genetic and environmental contributions.

faqs.question: How is CVID treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is lifelong immunoglobulin replacement by intravenous infusion every 3-4 weeks or subcutaneous injection weekly, targeting trough serum IgG above 7-8 g/L. Antibiotics treat breakthrough infections; rituximab and azathioprine treat granulomatous-lymphocytic lung disease and autoimmune cytopenias.

faqs.question: Can CVID be cured?
faqs.answer: CVID itself has no cure for polygenic disease, but lifelong immunoglobulin replacement reduces serious bacterial infections by 70-80% and supports near-normal life expectancy in patients without major non-infectious complications. Stem cell transplant is considered for selected severe monogenic forms.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy in CVID?
faqs.answer: Patients with infection-only CVID and adequate immunoglobulin replacement have near-normal life expectancy. Resnick et al. (Blood 2012) reported overall 35-year survival of 64% from diagnosis in adults. Autoimmune disease, granulomatous-lymphocytic lung disease, and lymphoma carry higher mortality.

faqs.question: Is CVID hereditary?
faqs.answer: CVID has a genetic component but is not always inherited. About 15-25% have identifiable monogenic mutations (autosomal dominant, recessive, or X-linked). First-degree relatives have 8-10% prevalence of antibody deficiency. Family screening with immunoglobulin testing is offered.

faqs.question: Can I have children with CVID?
faqs.answer: Yes. CVID does not affect fertility directly. Women with CVID continue immunoglobulin replacement during pregnancy at higher doses to maintain serum IgG. Pregnancy in CVID has good outcomes with multidisciplinary care; live vaccines are avoided in newborns of mothers on IVIG.

faqs.question: What vaccinations are safe in CVID?
faqs.answer: Inactivated vaccines (annual influenza, pneumococcal conjugate, COVID-19, pertussis, hepatitis A and B) are safe and recommended, though response may be limited. Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, yellow fever, BCG) are generally avoided. Vaccinate household contacts to provide indirect protection.

faqs.question: What is IVIG and how is it given?
faqs.answer: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is purified IgG from pooled donor plasma, given every 3-4 weeks at 400-600 mg/kg over 2-4 hours in a clinic or home setting. It replaces missing antibodies and reduces infection frequency. Side effects include headache, fatigue, and rarely renal or thrombotic events.

faqs.question: What is subcutaneous immunoglobulin?
faqs.answer: Subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) is given by weekly self-injection or every 2-4 weeks with facilitated SCIG (with hyaluronidase). It produces steadier serum IgG levels, fewer systemic reactions, and greater patient autonomy than IVIG. Most patients learn to self-administer at home after training.

faqs.question: Can CVID cause autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 20-30% of CVID patients develop autoimmune disease, most commonly immune thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, autoimmune thyroid disease, vitiligo, and pernicious anaemia. These often precede the infection phenotype by years and may be the presenting feature.

faqs.question: What is GLILD?
faqs.answer: Granulomatous-lymphocytic interstitial lung disease (GLILD) is a CVID-specific lung complication affecting 10-20% of patients. It produces granulomas and lymphoid infiltrates causing breathlessness and restrictive lung function. Treatment combines rituximab and azathioprine alongside immunoglobulin replacement.

faqs.question: Does CVID increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. CVID patients have approximately 4-fold increased lifetime risk of lymphoma (mostly non-Hodgkin B-cell) and significantly elevated gastric cancer risk. Routine surveillance with annual chest imaging and upper GI endoscopy in high-risk patients aids early detection.

faqs.question: How long does diagnosis take?
faqs.answer: The mean diagnostic delay is 4-9 years from first symptoms. Faster diagnosis comes from immunoglobulin testing in patients with more than 4 antibiotic courses per year, two pneumonias, recurrent sinusitis, or unexplained bronchiectasis. Family screening of first-degree relatives helps identify early disease.

faqs.question: Can children get CVID?
faqs.answer: Yes. Paediatric CVID is diagnosed after age 4 to allow normal immune maturation. Onset can be in childhood or adulthood. Management principles are the same but require careful attention to growth, vaccination schedules, and family-centred care.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CVID and selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: Selective IgA deficiency involves only low IgA with normal IgG and IgM and is usually asymptomatic. CVID involves low IgG plus low IgA or IgM with impaired vaccine response and recurrent infection. About 2-10% of IgA deficiency patients progress to CVID over years.

faqs.question: Can CVID be diagnosed in adults for the first time?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adult-onset CVID accounts for the majority of cases, with peak diagnosis between 20 and 45 years of age. Adults with recurrent infections, unexplained bronchiectasis, autoimmune cytopenia, or chronic diarrhoea should have immunoglobulin testing.

faqs.question: Are antibiotics still needed on immunoglobulin replacement?
faqs.answer: Yes. Immunoglobulin replacement reduces but does not eliminate infections. Breakthrough sinopulmonary infections require antibiotics. Patients with bronchiectasis or frequent exacerbations may benefit from prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin three times weekly).

faqs.question: What are the long-term complications of CVID?
faqs.answer: Long-term complications include bronchiectasis, granulomatous-lymphocytic lung disease, autoimmune cytopenias, enteropathy with malabsorption, lymphoma, gastric cancer, splenomegaly with hypersplenism, and chronic liver disease. Multidisciplinary surveillance and tailored treatment manage these complications.

symptomKeywords: more than 4 antibiotic courses a year
